---
title: "DA research"
author: "Nancy Tran"
output: html_document
date: "2025-07-18"
institute: "Denison University"
format: 
  html:
    toc: true
    theme: lux # Check here for more themes: https://quarto.org/docs/output-formats/html-themes.html
    code-tools: true
    code-fold: true
    code-summary: "Code"
    code-copy: hover
    link-external-newwindow: true
    tbl-cap-location: top
    fig-cap-location: bottom

self-contained: true
editor: source
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
# DO NOT EDIT THIS

knitr::opts_chunk$set(fig.align = 'center')
knitr::opts_chunk$set(out.width = '90%')
knitr::opts_chunk$set(results = 'hold')
knitr::opts_chunk$set(fig.show = 'hold')
knitr::opts_chunk$set(error = TRUE)
knitr::opts_chunk$set(warning = FALSE)
knitr::opts_chunk$set(message = FALSE)
par(mar = c(4.1, 4.1, 1.1, 4.1))

hooks = knitr::knit_hooks$get()
hook_foldable = function(type) {
  force(type)
  function(x, options) {
    res = hooks[[type]](x, options)
    
    if (isFALSE(options[[paste0("fold.", type)]])) return(res)
    
    paste0(
      "<details open><summary>", gsub("^p", "P", gsub("^o", "O", type)), "</summary>\n\n",
      res,
      "\n\n</details>"
    )
  }
}

knitr::knit_hooks$set(
  output = hook_foldable("output"),
  plot = hook_foldable("plot")
)

```

# Import Library

```{r}

library(dplyr)
library(stringr)
library(readr)
library(sf)
library(caret)
library(ranger)
library(xgboost)
library(Matrix)
library(ggplot2)
library(rgee)
library(cowplot)
library(geodata)
library(terra)
library(sf)
library(soilDB)
library(tidyr)
library(lme4)
library(lmerTest) 
library(performance)
```

# Dataset
```{r}
#Import dataset
soc_data <- read.csv("Data/clean1.csv", stringsAsFactors=FALSE)

#factor long and lat --> numeric
soc_data$lon<-as.numeric(soc_data$Longtitude)
soc_data$lat<-as.numeric(soc_data$Latitude)

#create longitude and latitude points for merging dataset 
pts_sf <- st_as_sf(soc_data, coords = c("lon","lat"),crs=4326) 
```

The pre-clean version of SOC data (from google sheet) has 4341 observations, 20 variables.

# Adding temperature and precipitation data from CHELSA portal (import from raster files)
```{r}
urls <- readLines("Data/envidatS3paths.txt")
target_urls <- urls[grepl("bio1_|bio12_", urls)]
for (u in target_urls) {
  dest <- file.path("data/chelsa", basename(u))
  if (!file.exists(dest)) download.file(u, dest, mode="wb")
}

# Load Chelsa Rasters
r_bio1 <-rast("../CHELSA_bio1_1981-2010_V.2.1.tif")
r_bio12 <- rast("../CHELSA_bio12_1981-2010_V.2.1.tif")

#Extract & merge with original data
bio <- c(r_bio1, r_bio12)
names(bio) <- c("bio1_temp", "bio12_precip")
vals <- terra::extract(bio, vect(pts_sf))
soc_data_climate <- cbind(soc_data, vals)
```

After the above code chunk, the annual average temperature and annual average 

# Checking depth varation of the dataset
```{r}
barplot(table(soc_data_climate$depth))
```
From the depth variation, we classify depth into 3 categories: 
- Depth 0-30cm
- Depth 30-60cm
- Depth 60+ cm
First, using the package 'stringr', we split the depth into lower and upper bound by taking the sum of the first measure and the last measure of the depth class, divided by 2, then classify them into 3 categories. 

```{r}
library(dplyr)
library(stringr)

soc_data_climate <- soc_data_climate %>%
  # split depth into lower/upper bounds
  mutate(depth_low  = as.numeric(str_extract(depth, "^[0-9]+")),
         depth_high = as.numeric(str_extract(depth, "[0-9]+$")),
         depth_mid  = (depth_high + depth_high) / 2) %>%
  # classify into bins
  mutate(depth_class = case_when(
    depth_mid <= 30 ~ "0-30",
    depth_mid > 30 & depth_mid <= 60 ~ "30-60",
    depth_mid > 60 ~ "60+",
    TRUE ~ NA_character_
  ))

#Checking distribution of depth class
barplot(table(soc_data_climate$depth_class))
```
The observation mostly still falls under 0-30cm depth. 
```{r}
table(gkg$depth_class)
```

# Elevation 
Data is extracted from Raster files from https://bigdata.cgiar.org/srtm-90m-digital-elevation-database/ 

```{r}
# the srtm files are downloaded from CSI CGAR (90m)
dem1<-rast("../srtm1.tif")
dem2<-rast("../srtm2.tif")
dem3<-rast("../srtm3.tif")
points<-data.frame(id=soc_data_climate$id,x=soc_data_climate$lon,y=soc_data_climate$lat)

pts_vec<-vect(points,geom=c("x","y"),crs="EPSG:4326")

e1 <- terra::extract(dem1, pts_vec)[,2]
e2 <- terra::extract(dem2, pts_vec)[,2]
e3 <- terra::extract(dem3, pts_vec)[,2]

# combine dem 1, 2, 3 into the elevation column
soc_data_climate$elev <- dplyr::coalesce(e1, e2, e3)

# add combine the two elevation column
soc_data_climate$average.elevation<-as.numeric(soc_data_climate$average.elevation)
soc_data_climate$elev <- coalesce(soc_data_climate$elev,
                                  soc_data_climate$average.elevation)
```

# Checking distribution of numeric continuous variables: precipitation, temperature, and elevation
```{r}
library(tidyr)
plot_data <- soc_data_climate %>%
  select(elev, bio1_temp, bio12_precip) %>%
  pivot_longer(cols = c(elev, bio1_temp, bio12_precip),
               names_to = "variable", values_to = "value")
# Facet Wrap them
ggplot(plot_data, aes(x = value)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 30) +
  facet_wrap(~variable, scales = "free") +
  theme_minimal()
```

At lower elevation with warmer temperatures, chemical weathering processes such as hydro and oxidation tend to me more rapid. This results in deeper soils rich in clay minerals, and secondary oxides --> more microbial activity, organic matter decomposes faster and is incorporated into mineral soil horizons


# Convert SOC unit because they are not consistent 

```{r}
# Convert SOC units into concentration (by mass)
soc_data_climate$SOC_gKg <- with(soc_data_climate,
  ifelse(soc.value_units == "%", soc.value * 10,
  ifelse(soc.value_units == "g_kg", soc.value,
  ifelse(soc.value_units == "g_g", soc.value * 1000,
  ifelse(soc.value_units == "mg_g", soc.value,                # 1 mg/g = 1 g/kg
  ifelse(soc.value_units == "mg_kg", soc.value / 1000, NA))))))
 
# Convert SOC units into stock (by area)
soc_data_climate$SOC_MgHa <- with(soc_data_climate,
  ifelse(soc.value_units == "Mg_ha", soc.value,
  ifelse(soc.value_units == "g_m2", soc.value * 0.01,   # 1 g/m² = 0.01 Mg/ha
  ifelse(soc.value_units == "kg_m2", soc.value * 10,    # 1 kg/m² = 10 Mg/ha
  ifelse(soc.value_units == "kg_ha", soc.value / 1000,  # 1 kg/ha = 0.001 Mg/ha
  NA)))))
```

# Log Transformation the SOC unit because there are super huge outliers

```{r}
library(dplyr)

# inspect
summary(soc_data_climate$SOC_MgHa)
summary(soc_data_climate$SOC_gKg)

# log transform function
safe_log <- function(x, add = NULL) {
  # add: if NULL, compute minimal shift so all values > 0
  if (is.null(add)) {
    minv <- min(x[is.finite(x)], na.rm = TRUE)
    # if minv <= 0, choose shift = abs(minv) + tiny
    add <- ifelse(minv <= 0, abs(minv) + 1e-6, 0)
  }
  log(x + add)
}

# create transformed variable
soc_unit <- soc_data_climate %>%
  mutate(
    SOC_MgHa_finite = ifelse(is.finite(SOC_MgHa), SOC_MgHa, NA_real_),
    SOC_gKg_finite = ifelse(is.finite(SOC_gKg), SOC_gKg, NA_real_)
  )
# Transform both SOC_MgHa and SOC_gKg
min_SOC_mgha <- min(soc_data$SOC_MgHa_finite, na.rm = TRUE)
min_SOC_gkg <- min(soc_data$SOC_gkg_finite, na.rm = TRUE)
shift_mgha <- ifelse(min_SOC_mgha <= 0, abs(min_SOC_mgha) + 1e-6, 0)
shift_gkg <- ifelse(min_SOC_gkg <= 0, abs(min_SOC_gkg) + 1e-6, 0)

soc_unit <- soc_unit %>%
  mutate(
    log_SOC_MgHa = safe_log(SOC_MgHa_finite, add = shift_mgha),
    log_SOC_gKg = safe_log(SOC_gKg_finite, add = shift_gkg)
  )
```

# Summary log measurement of SOC_MgHa (stock)
```{r}
summary(soc_unit$log_SOC_MgHa)
```

# Summary log measurement of SOC_gKg (concentration)
```{r}
summary(soc_unit$log_SOC_gKg)
```

# Secondary descriptor overtime

This is so ugly 
```{r}
soc_main$year<-as.numeric(soc_main$year)
soc_main %>%
  group_by(country, year) %>%
  summarise(mean_SOC = mean(log_SOC_gKg, na.rm = TRUE)) %>%
  ungroup() %>% 
  filter(!country %in% c("East Timor", "Myanmar")) %>% 
  ggplot(aes(x = year, y = mean_SOC)) +
  geom_line() + 
  # geom_point() +
  facet_wrap(~country) +
  labs(
    title = "SOC Concentration Over Time by Country",
    x = "Year",
    y = "Average ln(SOC) (g/kg)"
  ) +
  theme_light() +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", size = 20),
    axis.title = element_text(face = "bold", size = 14),
    axis.text.x = element_text(size = 10),
    axis.text.y = element_text(size = 10),
    # legend.position = "none",
    strip.text = element_text(face = "bold", size = 12)
  ) +
  scale_y_continuous(limits = c(0, 7), breaks = seq(0, 7, 2))


```

# 1. Testing mixed-effect model
```{r}
# Create another dataset called "soc_main", factoring the variables needed
soc_main <- soc_unit %>%
  mutate(
    depth_class = factor(depth_class, ordered = FALSE),
    main_descriptor = factor(main_descriptor),
    secondary_descriptor=factor(secondary_descriptor),
    additional_descriptor=factor(additional_descriptor),
    labmethod = factor(labmethod),
    crop=factor(crop),
    # scale continuous predictors for easier interpretation
    elevation_s = scale(elev),
    temp_s = scale(bio1_temp),
    precip_s = scale(bio12_precip)
  )

# log_SOC_MgHa measured by descriptors + site 
mod_lmm1 <- lmer(log_SOC_gKg ~ depth_class + temp_s + precip_s + labmethod + secondary_descriptor +
                  (1 | id), data = soc_main, REML = FALSE)

summary(mod_lmm1)
```

# making it as a summary table format
```{r}
mod_lmm1 <- lmer(log_SOC_gKg ~ depth_class + temp_s + precip_s + labmethod + secondary_descriptor +
                  (1 | id), data = soc_main, REML = FALSE)
install.packages("modelsummary")
library(modelsummary)
#Name the model
regz <- list(`SOC (g/kg)` = mod_lmm1)

#Change the variable name
coefz <- c(
  "(Intercept)" = "Constant",
  "depth_class30-60" = "Depth level 30-60cm",
  "depth_class60+" = "Depth level 60+cm",
  "temp_s" = "A.A. Temperature",
  "secondary_descriptorFertilization" = "Fertilization",
  "labmethodLoss on ignition" = "Lab method - Loss on ignition"
  )

#Select the statistic to show in the table 
gofz <- c("nobs", "r.squared", "adj.r.squared")

model_summary <- modelsummary(
  regz,
  estimate = c("{estimate}{stars}"),
  gof_map = gofz,
  coef_map = coefz,
  title = "Effect of depth, climate, land management on SOC (g/kg)"
  )

model_summary
```

```{r}
# residuals, qq-plot
plot(mod_lmm1)    # residuals vs fitted
qqnorm(resid(mod_lmm1)); qqline(resid(mod_lmm1))
```

```{r}
# check collinearity
check_collinearity(mod_lmm1)
```
SOC (g/kg) declines with depth, temperature, and elevation (though elevation and temperature might be correlated). 
Fertilization appears to enhance SOC
Annual cropping likely reduces SOC, though not strongly in this dataset. 
There is unobserved site-level vairance (random effect on id).

Causal hypothesis based on mixed models effect (random effects on site)
- Climate --> Land use & management --> SOC: temperature and precipitation influence cropping system and fertilization use, land management and soil depth affect SOC. 

Conceptually, each arrow represent a submodel:
- temp&precip --> secondary_descriptor
- depth_class --> SOC
- secondary_descriptor --> SOC

## 2. Set up SEM
#Create dummy variable for secondary_descriptors --> this needs to be done differently beacause of so many different categories in this. 
```{r}

fert_data <- soc_main %>%
  dplyr::filter(grepl("Fertilization", secondary_descriptor, ignore.case = TRUE))

fert_data <- fert_data %>%
  dplyr::mutate(
    secondary_descriptor_Fertilization = ifelse(
      grepl("without", additional_descriptor, ignore.case = TRUE), 0,
      ifelse(grepl("with|fertilizer|mg|kg|N|application", additional_descriptor, ignore.case = TRUE), 1, NA)
    )
  )

table(fert_data$secondary_descriptor_Fertilization)

```

```{r}
gkg <- subset(fert_data, !is.na(log_SOC_gKg))
mgha <-subset(fert_data,!is.na(log_SOC_MgHa))
table(gkg$secondary_descriptor_Fertilization)
```

```{r}
library(nlme)
library(piecewiseSEM)

m1 <- lmer(secondary_descriptor_Fertilization ~ temp_s + precip_s + elevation_s + (1|id), data = gkg)

m2 <- lmer(log_SOC_gKg ~ depth_class + elevation_s + temp_s + precip_s + secondary_descriptor_Fertilization + (1|id), data = gkg)

#m3 <- lmer(log_SOC_gKg ~ secondary_descriptor_Fertilization + lab_method + depth_class + (1|id), data = gkg )

sem_model <- psem(m1,m2)
summary(sem_model)

```


Fisher's C test: p=0.256 --> non-significant
- This means the model fits the data well - the hypothesized causal structure (climate --> fertilization --> SOC) is consistent with the observed data. In SEM terms: there are no "missing" relationships that the model failed to capture.

Conceptually this model confirms that climate and elevation influence fertilization behavior, which in turn influences SOC. The over systems fits the observed data (p>0.05).

```{r}
table(gkg$depth_class)
car::vif(lm(temp_s ~ elevation_s + precip_s, data = gkg))
length(unique(gkg$id))
gkg$log_SOC_gkg <-as.numeric(gkg$log_SOC_gKg)
hist(gkg$log_SOC_gkg, main="Distribution of SOC (g/kg)", col="skyblue", breaks=50)
#barplot(table(gkg$log_SOC_gkg), main="Distribution of depth (cm)",col="skyblue")

gkg %>% 
  ggplot(aes(log_SOC_gKg)) +
  geom_histogram() +
  theme_light()

```

## Move on from SEM to Random Forest
```{r}
library(randomForest)

rf_model <- randomForest(
  log_SOC_gKg ~ depth_class + elevation_s + temp_s + precip_s + secondary_descriptor_Fertilization,
  data = gkg,
  na.action = na.omit,
  importance = TRUE,
  ntree = 500
)

# Use explicit namespace call
randomForest::importance(rf_model)
randomForest::varImpPlot(rf_model)

```
#Visualization of variable importance
```{r}
importance_df <- as.data.frame(randomForest::importance(rf_model))
importance_df$Variable <- rownames(importance_df)

library(ggplot2)
ggplot(importance_df, aes(x = reorder(Variable, `%IncMSE`), y = `%IncMSE`)) +
  geom_col(fill = "#2E8B57") +
  coord_flip() +
  labs(
    title = "Variable Importance in Predicting SOC (g/kg)",
    x = "Predictor",
    y = "Increase in MSE (%) if Variable is Permuted"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 14)
```

# Partial Dependence Plots

```{r}
library(pdp)
pdp_elev <- partial(rf_model, pred.var = "elev.s", plot = TRUE, main = "Effect of Elevation on SOC")
pdp_elev
pdp_temp <- partial(rf_model, pred.var = "temp_s", plot = TRUE, main = "Effect of Temperature on SOC")
pdp_temp

```

# Example of a decision tree visualization
```{r}

library(rpart)
library(rpart.plot)

# Example: your random forest model
rf_model <- randomForest(
  log_SOC_gKg ~ depth_class + elevation_s + temp_s + precip_s + secondary_descriptor_Fertilization,
  data = gkg,
  na.action = na.omit,
  importance = TRUE,
  ntree = 500
)

# Extract ONE tree (e.g., the 10th tree) from the forest
tree10 <- getTree(rf_model, k = 10, labelVar = TRUE)

# Convert it to rpart object for plotting
# First, create a small training subset to match structure
fit_rpart <- rpart(
  log_SOC_gKg ~ depth_class + elevation_s + temp_s + precip_s + secondary_descriptor_Fertilization,
  data = gkg,
  method = "anova"
)

# Visualize decision structure
rpart.plot(fit_rpart, type = 2, extra = 101, fallen.leaves = TRUE,
           main = "Example Decision Tree for Predicting SOC (g/kg)")

```

## EDA Code
# Distribution of published years of articles
```{r}
barplot(table(soc_data_climate$year))
```

# Distribution of experiment year
```{r}
barplot(table(soc_data_climate$exp_year))
```

# Distribution of country's article
```{r}
ggplot(soc_data_climate, aes(x = country)) +
  geom_bar()+theme_minimal()
```

# Distribution of crops
won't use crop as a variable in research
```{r}
crop_counts <- soc_data_climate %>%
  count(crop, sort = TRUE)

ggplot(crop_counts %>% slice_max(n, n = 20), aes(x = reorder(crop, n), y = n)) +
  geom_col(fill = "steelblue") +
  coord_flip() +
  labs(x = "Crop / System", y = "Count", title = "Top 20 Reported Crops/Systems") +
  theme_minimal()
```

# distribution of SOC_MgHa and SOC_gKg before log transformation
```{r}
soc <- soc_data_climate %>%
  select(SOC_gKg, SOC_MgHa) %>%
  pivot_longer(cols = everything(), 
               names_to = "variable", 
               values_to = "value")
ggplot(soc, aes(x = variable, y = value)) +
  geom_boxplot() +
  labs(x = "SOC Variable", y = "Value", title = "SOC distributions") +
  theme_minimal()

```
